title: @pure hides ! Alloc[Heap] from no-alloc callers — silent abort/corruption risk
description: A function marked @pure can allocate internally on Heap. When called from a no-alloc context (given [A: Allocator where A: NoAlloc]) the call compiles because @pure suppresses ! Alloc from the visible effect set. At runtime the function may call malloc, which fails or corrupts. The spec explicitly acknowledges this (ferrum-lang-ownership.md §2.4): 'may allocate at runtime → abort or corruption' and calls it 'not a design bug'. But it is: a language feature (@pure) that compiles no-alloc callers that will abort or corrupt is a type safety failure. The @pure guarantee should be split into two orthogonal properties: @pure (referentially transparent, no observable effects) and @no-alloc (no allocation). A no-alloc context should only accept functions with @no-alloc, not @pure.
